why_it_matters: A candidate that silently excludes a difficult representation or machine boundary is smaller only by changing the problem.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: one fixed logic can state every registered family and future protocols that reduce to it. Liability: expressibility is only a paper claim until proof obligations normalize and cleanup is derivable. Trade-off: widest semantic reach for the largest feasibility uncertainty.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: all 15 gaps route through a finite orthogonal algebra. Liability: a future topology, footprint, lifecycle, or cleanup form may not factor into that algebra. Trade-off: bounded coverage with explicit findings instead of open-ended proof power.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: every current gap has a named family route with locally explicit operations. Liability: coverage is fitted most closely to the frozen witnesses and new shapes require language additions. Trade-off: strongest local concreteness for weakest extrapolation.
evidence_status: Paper routing only; 15/15 bookkeeping is verified, exact D-2 and deferred platform rows remain open.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: A candidate loses coverage credit when one held-out needs an excluded workload, a new trusted route, asymptotic regression, unavoidable pathological storage, or an unenumerated machine event.

why_it_matters: The objective is to reach the requested layout, traffic, checks, calls, and machine events without unsafe or hidden work.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: erased proofs can authorize arbitrary representation-selected metadata and direct moves. Liability: proof normalization and derived cleanup may create code or inhibit optimization. Trade-off: representation freedom depends on reliable proof erasure and fact lowering.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: tag-free storage and direct fixed place transitions align closely with native loads, stores, relocation, and topology-specific metadata. Liability: closed forms may force adaptation when the shape is not canonical. Trade-off: predictable primitive lowering for bounded representation freedom.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: each family can promise a canonical lowering and tune its representation directly. Liability: conversion between families or an unlisted algorithm can add copies, allocation, or indirection. Trade-off: best known-shape control at the cost of general representation choice.
evidence_status: Existing bounds, alias, and law channels prove that verified facts can change code shape; no candidate-specific code exists.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Reject a zero-cost claim if structural accounting or optimized bodies show extra initialization, zeroing, copy, move, allocation, metadata access, indirection, call, check, or machine event relative to the contract.

why_it_matters: A universal mechanism is unacceptable if fixed, append-only, unique, movable, portable, or non-JIT code pays for stronger abilities it did not request.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: indices and proofs erase, so stronger state need not alter weaker representation. Liability: proof parameters, monomorphization, cleanup, or fact plumbing may still grow code. Trade-off: no runtime tax is plausible, while compile-time and code-size tax are major risks.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: each topology/lifecycle form carries only selected counters or metadata and protected forms remain distinct. Liability: generic APIs may accidentally widen to a stronger form or introduce conversion checks. Trade-off: explicit form selection makes taxes auditable.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: fixed and P2 families can be separately frozen with exact layouts. Liability: shared helper code or cross-family wrappers may import another family's headers and branches. Trade-off: easiest local pins, hardest global cross-family discipline.
evidence_status: NT-FIXED and NT-P2 are protected controls; candidate parity is untested.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Any metadata, load, branch, indirection, call, fact dependency, allocation, atomic, pin, target dispatch, or image-lifecycle event added to an unselected weaker route is a falsifier.

why_it_matters: Rule count affects specification size, checker defects, diagnostics, AI learnability, and the chance that similar operations acquire inconsistent semantics.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: a small number of general concepts can replace many topology-specific rules. Liability: each concept has a deep semantic definition and interaction matrix. Trade-off: low headline count but high rule power and proof-language surface.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: a finite algebra offers medium-sized, table-driven rules with shared transition semantics. Liability: each newly discovered orthogonal form expands the tables and combinations. Trade-off: moderate count and moderate semantic depth.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: each family rule is local and concrete. Liability: the total number of operations, state machines, and pairwise composition rules is largest. Trade-off: shallow individual rules for broad surface area.
evidence_status: Inventory counts exist: 10 A-specific, 11 B-specific, 12 C-specific items plus 11 common items; normative rule counts are unknown.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Count normative judgments, state transitions, cross-product cases, diagnostics, and target clauses rather than syntax tokens; reject any candidate whose normal-frontend budget is exceeded.

why_it_matters: Ordinary checked libraries must build new containers and systems policies without compiler or standard-library privilege.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: new protocols compose whenever their proofs reduce to the fixed logic. Liability: composition creates the hardest formulas, provenance products, and cleanup proofs. Trade-off: strongest library autonomy with the highest proof burden.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: ordinary algorithms combine storage, topology, footprints, handles, lifecycle, and cleanup forms. Liability: composition stops at the closed algebra boundary. Trade-off: useful autonomy without user-defined proof rules.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: libraries can wrap and combine admitted families with predictable behavior. Liability: new low-level protocols and many cross-family combinations require compiler changes. Trade-off: least library autonomy for simplest local checking.
evidence_status: Paper derivations exist; exact witness and held-out derivations are pending.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: A candidate loses if a held-out needs a new privileged named container, compiler-only algorithm, trusted callback, standard-library-only raw access, or quadratic cross-family rules.

why_it_matters: The standing design permits normal-compiler-frontend effort, not rustc-scale inference or an unbounded theorem prover.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: one proof kernel centralizes soundness. Liability: finite maps, quantified owned ranges, provenance, versions, cleanup, and happens-before may make checking or normalization intractable. Trade-off: small trusted kernel with potentially enormous elaboration and proof workloads.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: closed state tables, scoped flow, and exact exits are implementable as bounded dataflow. Liability: the seams between topology, ownership, provenance, cleanup, and facts remain soundness hotspots. Trade-off: normal-front-end plausibility depends on keeping every algebra closed.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: each operation checks through a small family state machine. Liability: total checker code and cross-family consistency obligations are large. Trade-off: easiest local judgments but largest repeated implementation surface.
evidence_status: Current simplified ownership checker has 0 model-check violations but these extensions are unmodeled.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Use preregistered rule count, asymptotic analysis, compile-time/resource ceilings, mutation tests, and independent model checking; exceeding any bound or accepting one hostile state falsifies feasibility or soundness.

why_it_matters: Special cases raise implementation and regression cost and can make performance target-specific or fragile.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: most accepted operations lower through generic typed memory and fact interfaces. Liability: proof erasure, cleanup synthesis, and fact normalization are sophisticated compiler passes. Trade-off: fewer operation-specific lowerings but deeper generic infrastructure.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: direct place operations and topology views map to a bounded lowering table. Liability: every topology, footprint, lifecycle, and code-shape form still needs frontend-to-backend parity. Trade-off: medium breadth and medium depth.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: the compiler can provide canonical optimized lowering per family operation. Liability: it owns the largest operation table, backend matrix, and regression corpus. Trade-off: maximum control of known shapes for maximum maintenance burden.
evidence_status: No candidate implementation; current scalar and fact-channel parity only show the verification style is possible.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Compare implementation sites, lowering rows, target-specific branches, negative mutations, and optimized-body parity; backend growth or fragile target divergence can eliminate a candidate.

why_it_matters: Headers, tags, bitmaps, generations, refcounts, leases, locks, and descriptors are direct memory, cache, branch, and update costs.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: proof state erases and libraries store only representation-required metadata. Liability: dynamic proof producers still need validation state and shared protocols need concrete metadata. Trade-off: minimum theoretical metadata with weak implementation evidence.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: forms state exactly which counters/control bytes/generations/counts exist and do not impose one universal descriptor. Liability: Sparse and shared forms may standardize metadata that a specialized representation could compress. Trade-off: auditable bounded metadata for less freedom.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: each family selects a tuned representation. Liability: wrappers and cross-family composition can duplicate headers or retain redundant state. Trade-off: local tuning can lose globally.
evidence_status: Witness budgets identify forbidden metadata; no candidate structural accounting is complete.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: For every witness, enumerate permanent and transient bytes, loads, stores, branches, atomics, scans, and version updates; any unexplained item fails the row.

why_it_matters: Recoverable failure, partial progress, callback outcomes, and abandonment are where owner loss, double drop, leaks, rollback traffic, and hidden copies appear.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: pre/post proofs can state exact branch-specific disposition. Liability: proof-directed destruction may be non-unique, code-heavy, or difficult to make total. Trade-off: most expressive failure algebra for the hardest cleanup synthesis.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: fixed topology enumeration and scoped protocols give canonical cleanup and commit points. Liability: callback-dependent or recursive partial progress may not fit fixed cleanup. Trade-off: predictable common cases with explicit incompleteness risk.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: every family operation can freeze exact branch and cleanup behavior. Liability: similar operations may diverge across families and every new family repeats the audit. Trade-off: concrete local contracts for broad duplicated review.
evidence_status: Ownership research records exact branch distinctions; no candidate safety model exists.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Hostile cases must cover offered-owner return, zero/one/many callback calls, partial clone, overlap, early stop, abandonment, cancellation, trap-before-invalid-access, and exact live-slot destruction.

why_it_matters: Checks stay on unless machine proof discharges them; facts must also prevent guard-heavy or scalar code shapes without changing semantics.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: the resource logic can express the richest propositions and feed the fixed fact interface. Liability: proposition transfer and invalidation complexity is highest and may expose optimizer-unsound seams. Trade-off: maximum optimization opportunity with maximum fact review burden.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: fixed state and footprint forms produce a closed, auditable fact vocabulary. Liability: profitable future facts outside that vocabulary require a language change. Trade-off: bounded authority and bounded optimization reach.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: every family operation can emit precise known facts and guaranteed shapes. Liability: facts become fragmented and cross-family transfer can be inconsistent or missing. Trade-off: strongest local facts for weakest generality.
evidence_status: Scoped alias, checked law, and bounds proof have measured wins; future state/identity/shared/refinement/dispatch facts are unmeasured.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: For each fact record proposition, owner/root/version, producer, consumer, invalidators, transfer, speculation, facts-off identity, artifact evidence, and negative canaries; one stale or forgeable fact eliminates the channel.

why_it_matters: AI is the primary writer; success requires predictable construction, bounded diagnostics, and reliable repair without unsafe escape.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: one uniform logic may support systematic proof generation. Liability: long proof terms, abstraction, solver dependence, and nonlocal failures are likely hard for lower-tier models. Trade-off: conceptual uniformity versus high generation burden.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: a closed pattern catalog gives finite choices and diagnostics can name one missing form or transition. Liability: combinations and exact-exit repair remain nonlocal. Trade-off: strongest current hypothesis for bounded AI behavior, without evidence.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: local family APIs and state errors are concrete. Liability: the writer must choose among many families and may convert or combine them pathologically. Trade-off: easiest local completion with global architecture risk.
evidence_status: No AI protocol has been run or authorized.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Pre-register first-green construction and repair tasks over visible plus held-out structures; measure success, retries, source size, wrong-family selection, hidden-cost mistakes, and diagnostic locality.

why_it_matters: A compact law set and closed taught patterns reduce ambiguity, drift, and model confusion.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: few top-level abstractions could produce a compact core. Liability: the formal logic, proof terms, normalization, and interaction laws make the true specification deep. Trade-off: syntactic compactness may hide semantic bulk.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: a finite matrix can be specified and taught as a bounded set of patterns. Liability: six topology forms plus footprints, lifecycle, cleanup, facts, and combinations are still substantial. Trade-off: moderate visible and semantic size.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: each family has an intuitive local story. Liability: the aggregate manual, operation matrix, and family-selection doctrine are largest. Trade-off: approachable pages individually, sprawling language globally.
evidence_status: No draft normative text or teaching trial exists.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Count normative rules, cross references, examples, negative rules, and taught choices; test whether lower-tier models can select and explain the correct route without hidden costs.

why_it_matters: Systems code must retain the abilities with no privileged standard library; ordinary libraries should define policy and containers.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: proof-indexed protocols are maximally available to ordinary modules. Liability: practical use may depend on proof combinator libraries even if the kernel is sufficient. Trade-off: semantic independence with possible usability dependence.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: the orthogonal substrate is compiler-provided while all named containers and policies remain ordinary modules. Liability: missing convenience combinators can make direct use verbose. Trade-off: strong no-stdlib fit if the kernel remains truly orthogonal.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: family substrates remain available without named standard containers. Liability: the compiler prelude effectively becomes a large low-level library frozen into the language. Trade-off: technically no-stdlib, operationally compiler-owned breadth.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Build each witness in an empty package importing only kernel/prelude capabilities; any hidden helper, privileged container, generated standard module, or trusted library body fails the claim.

why_it_matters: A general systems language must define which programs are portable and make target-specific costs and rejection explicit.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: abstract resource proofs are target-neutral and exact event tables isolate machine differences. Liability: proof success, layout, and cleanup code may still vary by target. Trade-off: strongest semantic portability with complex lowering equivalence.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: storage and transition forms are mostly target-neutral and exact tables make unsupported events reject. Liability: topology and atomic forms may have target-specific efficient subsets. Trade-off: explicit portability with bounded variant handling.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: families can be optimized per target. Liability: the family/backend matrix is largest and semantic or performance parity can drift. Trade-off: best per-target specialization for highest portability maintenance.
evidence_status: Existing target and external rows are unenumerated.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Require identical source semantics, explicit target rejection, per-target structural event accounts, and parity tests; silent fallback, extra events, or semantic drift falsifies portability.

why_it_matters: The language must solve future performance barriers without an uncontrolled stream of keywords, intrinsics, facts, or family APIs.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: many future protocols need no language change if the fixed logic can express them. Liability: pressure will shift toward extending the logic, whose additions are extremely powerful. Trade-off: infrequent but high-impact extension decisions.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: additions must demonstrate one missing orthogonal form and its no-tax behavior. Liability: the closed algebra may accrete forms gradually and interactions can still grow. Trade-off: visible controlled growth with a permanent completeness audit.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: adding a family solves one concrete workload directly. Liability: each new workload invites another family or operation and surface growth is the default response. Trade-off: responsive specialization with the greatest loss-of-control risk.
evidence_status: Paper comparison only; historical family and privilege research supplies warnings, not candidate evidence.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Every proposed addition must carry multiple independent removal witnesses, proof it is not derivable, rule and interaction deltas, no-tax accounting, and a held-out; repeated additions that fail orthogonality eliminate the design.

why_it_matters: All performance authority intersects initialization, ownership, provenance, destruction, interference, or optimizer correctness; review cost is a primary correctness cost.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: one proof kernel offers a concentrated soundness target. Liability: its logic, elaboration, proof transfer, and cleanup semantics have the widest attack surface. Trade-off: small trusted core does not imply a small review problem.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: closed forms permit exhaustive transition and mutation matrices. Liability: the type/flow/fact seams remain numerous and additions reopen cross-product review. Trade-off: best prospect for bounded hostile review if the algebra stays small.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: each family can be independently model-checked. Liability: cross-family products and duplicated semantics multiply adversarial cases. Trade-off: local auditability with global inconsistency risk.
evidence_status: Current ownership and proof tiers have hostile-review practice; none of these candidate extensions has been modeled.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Reject on one accepted uninitialized read, owner duplication/loss, wrong-root borrow, duplicate/missed drop, stale identity, race, stale fact, unchecked event, or source-forged authority; compare total mutation and model state spaces.

why_it_matters: A zero-runtime-cost design can still fail if proofs, monomorphization, cleanup, adapters, or family lowering make builds or binaries impractical.
candidate_a_proof_indexed: Advantage: erased proofs avoid runtime data. Liability: proof checking, normalization, specialization, and generated cleanup have the highest blowup risk. Trade-off: runtime generality paid at compile time and possibly in code size.
candidate_b_bounded_kernel: Advantage: bounded forms and direct operations permit predictable compilation and shared lowerings. Liability: combinations and monomorphized stateful callables can still multiply code. Trade-off: most controllable hypothesis for balanced build and binary size.
candidate_c_family_specialized: Advantage: prebuilt family operations may share code or inline canonical bodies. Liability: many family variants and target lowerings enlarge the compiler/runtime and inlining may duplicate operations. Trade-off: low proof cost for high implementation and binary breadth.
evidence_status: The 17x guard-versioning code-size result proves code shape matters; no candidate build exists.
decision_rule_or_falsifier: Pre-register compiler CPU/memory, proof or transition count, generic instantiations, optimized instruction bytes, and adapter growth as a function of depth; superlinear unexplained growth falsifies the claim.
